content:
8 min read
1 hour ago

--

Part 1 covered vectors, matrices, dot products, independence, rank, and projection. This part is about what happens once your directions are crooked, and why anyone bothers fixing that.
Real data is never as clean as two arrows pointing in obviously different directions.
Usually your directions overlap a little. Lean into each other. Quietly break the math without announcing it. This part is about fixing that, and the two places in AI where the fix pays for itself immediately: giant text embeddings, and why fine-tuning a huge model got cheap.
Save This Glossary First
Basis: a small set of directions that can build everything else, with nobody redundant in the group.
Orthonormal basis: a basis where every direction is also perfectly perpendicular and the same length.
Gram-Schmidt: the process of straightening crooked directions into an orthonormal basis.
QR decomposition: splitting a matrix into a clean basis (Q) and a recipe card (R) for rebuilding the original.
Eigenvector: a direction a matrix stretches but never bends.
Basis: Three Paint Colors
Grab three paint colors. Red, yellow, blue. Mix them in different amounts, and you can make basically any color you can think of. Purple, orange, brown, all of it, just from these three.
That’s a basis.
But here’s the real test of whether a set of colors counts as a basis. Take away just one of the three. Say, blue. Suddenly there are colors you can never make again, no matter how you mix red and yellow. That gap proves blue was pulling real weight. It wasn’t sitting there for decoration.
So a basis is really just this: a small set of ingredients where every single one is necessary, and together they’re enough to build anything else in that space. Lose one, and something becomes permanently unreachable. Add a redundant one that’s secretly just a mix of the others, and it wasn’t really adding anything new to begin with, it was just riding along.
Red, yellow, blue happens to pass both sides of that test. Necessary, and enough.
Your screen is doing the exact same trick right now, just with a different set of three. Red, green, blue light, each measured from 0 to 255. Mix those three in different amounts, and you get every single color your screen has ever shown you. Pure red is [255, 0, 0]. Purple is [128, 0, 128]. Over 16 million colors, all built from just three numbers. Take away green, and there are colors your screen can never display again, same gap as pulling blue out of the paint set. Different three ingredients, same exact idea.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Gram-Schmidt: Fixing A Crooked Ruler
Say you’re measuring a table with two rulers. Ruler one measures width. Straight, no problem. Ruler two measures height, except it’s tilted a little, leaning toward the width direction.
Now your height number is lying to you. Some of what it measures is secretly just width, sneaking in sideways. Change the table’s width, and somehow the height reading shifts too, even though the table never got any taller.
Here’s the entire fix, in three moves.
Ruler one is [1, 0]. Leave it exactly where it is. Ruler two is [1, 1], tilted. First, find the part of ruler two that overlaps with ruler one, its shadow. That works out to [1, 0]. Second, subtract the shadow from ruler two. [1, 1] minus [1, 0] leaves [0, 1]. Third, that's it. You're done. [0, 1] is guaranteed perpendicular to [1, 0], because you already scooped out every bit of overlap there was to scoop.
Got a third crooked ruler in the mix? Same trick again. Subtract its shadow onto both rulers you already fixed, one after another. Whatever survives is clean too.
This isn’t just a party trick either. A cheap accelerometer’s three axes are rarely perfectly perpendicular in the actual hardware. Tiny manufacturing drift leans one reading into another. Engineers run this exact shadow-subtraction on the raw sensor readings before trusting them. Same process, just fixing physical measurements instead of arrows on paper. Skip that step, and the device quietly reports the wrong orientation forever.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

QR: The Recipe Card
Run that same shadow-subtracting trick on every direction in a whole matrix at once, and you get two honest pieces back instead of one messy pile.
Q is the clean, straightened basis. The fixed rulers. R is the recipe card, exactly how much of each clean ruler you needed to rebuild the crooked originals. Multiply Q and R back together, you get your original crooked matrix again. Just now you also have the clean version and the recipe sitting separately, instead of tangled into one mess.
This does real work in three places. Solving equations more reliably than the usual textbook method, because crooked overlapping directions let a tiny measuring mistake balloon into a big wrong answer, the way a splash of vinegar secretly also adds saltiness if your ingredients overlap. Finding a matrix’s favorite directions, coming up next, by repeating the clean-up over and over like slowly bringing a blurry photo into focus. Fitting the best line through messy data, which is secretly the shadow problem again, and shadows only measure honestly against clean rulers.
Rank tells this whole process how many real rulers to expect before it even starts. Go looking for three clean directions when your data only actually has two, and the shadow-subtraction hands you back a leftover ruler of basically zero length. That’s not a bug. That’s the math politely telling you the third direction was never really there.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Orthonormal Basis: The Joystick That Never Betrays You
Ever play a game where pushing the joystick straight up somehow also nudges your character sideways? Deeply annoying. That happens when up-down and left-right aren’t perfectly separated. One leaks into the other a little.
An orthonormal basis is a joystick that never does that. Push straight up, you go straight up. No sideways surprise, ever.
The rotation machine from Part 1 is secretly a perfect example of one. Its two rows are always exactly perpendicular to each other, always exactly the same length, no matter what angle you rotate by. That’s precisely why rotating something never stretches or distorts it. Lengths and angles come out completely unharmed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Eigenvectors: The Arrows That Refuse To Bend
Stretch a rubber sheet harder along one line than any other direction. Most arrows drawn on that sheet end up tilted, dragged partway toward the stretch.
A couple of arrows don’t tilt at all. The ones already pointing exactly along the stretch line, or exactly against it. They only get longer or shorter, never bent off course. Those are eigenvectors. The stubborn directions a matrix only scales, never rotates. How much they get scaled by is called the eigenvalue.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Once you know those two, you can describe the entire matrix’s behavior using just them, instead of tracking what happens to every possible arrow one at a time. Google’s original PageRank ran on exactly this idea. Passing “importance” around a giant network of links, over and over, until it settles into one stable pattern that stops shifting no matter how many more times you repeat it. That settled, stable pattern is the eigenvector. Page importance itself, sitting right there in the math.
This also quietly answers something from Part 1. PCA, mentioned back in the projection section, finds the one direction your data actually spreads out along the most, then projects everything onto just that direction, throwing the rest away on purpose. That “direction of most spread” is nothing more than the eigenvector with the biggest eigenvalue. Rank told you how many real directions exist. Projection told you how to collapse onto fewer of them. Eigenvectors tell you exactly which ones are worth keeping. Three ideas that felt separate. Actually one technique.
Why 768 Numbers, Not Just One
Guess my favorite person using one single fact. Their height. Impossible. Plenty of people share a height and are nothing alike.
Play 20 Questions instead. Height, age, favorite music, sense of humor, how loud they laugh. Twenty small facts, and now two people scoring similarly across all of them are probably genuinely alike.
That’s exactly why a word inside an AI model gets 768 numbers instead of one. Each number is a tiny learned fact. Something like how royal a word feels, how formal, what part of speech it tends to be. One number can’t hold all of that. Hundreds can.
Nobody decided these 768 questions in advance either. The model built its own version of 20 Questions by reading enormous amounts of text, nudging every number slightly every single time it got something wrong. 768 itself isn’t some universal constant. Just a size that balanced “enough facts to tell words apart” against “not so much that everything gets slow,” for one particular model. Other models pick different sizes, chasing the exact same tradeoff.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Where This Pays Off: LoRA
Fine-tuning a giant model the normal way means adjusting a matrix that might be 4096 by 4096. Over 16 million individual numbers, for one single layer.
Repacking your entire house for a weekend trip. Nobody does that. You pack a small bag with just what changed, leave the house exactly where it is.
LoRA does exactly that for AI. Instead of touching all 16 million numbers directly, it bets the real update only lives in a small, low-rank slice, maybe 16 dimensions worth, and learns two small matrices instead of one giant one, multiplying them together only when needed to approximate the full update.
The math behind why this saves so much: 4096 times 4096 is nearly 16.8 million numbers. Two small matrices, one 4096 by 16 and one 16 by 4096, add up to about 131,000 numbers. Roughly 128 times fewer numbers to actually train. Rank, from Part 1, used on purpose this time. Not showing up as an accident nobody wanted.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why Any Of This Actually Matters
Next time you see “QK transpose,” a rank-16 adapter, or a similarity score in a search tool, none of it reads as alien symbols anymore.
Two flashlights being compared. A shadow being measured. A crooked ruler, straightened out with nothing but subtraction.
That’s the difference between pushing buttons on an unlabeled controller, and knowing exactly what every single button does.
Key Sources

• Rohit G., “Linear Algebra Intuition”, AI Engineering From Scratch
